boundary: Compartment rows separate supported pharmacokinetic evidence from tissue-local hypotheses. Plasma or urine biomarkers are not proof of every tissue distribution.

rows.inheritedNullClaim: A no-function genotype or whole-gene deletion can remove or sharply reduce CYP2D6-mediated metabolism at the main systemic clearance site.
rows.inducedNullClaim: A strong inhibitor can create a functional null-like state for hepatic/systemic CYP2D6 substrates while the inhibitor is present.
rows.localVsSystemic: This is the usual pharmacogenomics frame, but it is not the whole gene.
rows.boundary: Do not convert compartment evidence into personal diagnosis or medication advice.

rows.inheritedNullClaim: A true null may remove any local CYP2D6 contribution at the gut-entry layer, while systemic hepatic metabolism remains the dominant tested frame for most substrates.
rows.inducedNullClaim: An inhibitor may suppress intestinal or hepatic contribution depending on local exposure, timing, and inhibitor distribution.
rows.localVsSystemic: Food, microbiome, absorption, transporters, and first-pass metabolism can change which pathway gets loaded before plasma measurements appear.
rows.boundary: Do not convert compartment evidence into personal diagnosis or medication advice.

rows.evidenceKind: human expression or functional tissue evidence
rows.inheritedNullClaim: A true null may remove CYP2D6 function wherever CYP2D6 would normally be expressed in the brain.
rows.inducedNullClaim: An inhibitor may or may not reproduce the same brain state; brain penetration, local concentration, transporter behavior, and regional regulation matter.
rows.localVsSystemic: Brain CYP expression and regulation can differ from hepatic expression and regulation.
rows.boundary: Do not convert compartment evidence into personal diagnosis or medication advice.

rows.inheritedNullClaim: Inherited null status may affect local metabolism at CNS interfaces only where the enzyme is present and relevant.
rows.inducedNullClaim: A drug-induced null depends on whether the inhibitor reaches the barrier or CNS compartment in enough concentration.
rows.localVsSystemic: Barrier transport and local biotransformation can make central exposure diverge from plasma exposure.
rows.boundary: Do not convert compartment evidence into personal diagnosis or medication advice.

rows.inheritedNullClaim: A true null can reduce CYP2D6-derived metabolite formation that later appears in urine, even when the local renal role is not the main mechanism.
rows.inducedNullClaim: A strong inhibitor can lower CYP2D6-derived urinary metabolite ratios while inhibition persists.
rows.localVsSystemic: Urine is often a readout of upstream metabolism, not proof that kidney-local CYP2D6 is the causal compartment.
rows.boundary: Do not convert compartment evidence into personal diagnosis or medication advice.

rows.inheritedNullClaim: A CYP2D6 null can change exposure to cardiovascular drugs such as selected beta-blockers or antiarrhythmics.
rows.inducedNullClaim: A strong CYP2D6 inhibitor can create similar systemic exposure changes for susceptible cardiovascular substrates.
rows.localVsSystemic: This is often target-exposure biology rather than proof of local cardiac CYP2D6 loss; the atlas should label that distinction.
rows.boundary: Do not convert compartment evidence into personal diagnosis or medication advice.

rows.inheritedNullClaim: A true null may increase parent exposure for lipophilic CYP2D6 substrates before distribution into or release from lipid compartments.
rows.inducedNullClaim: An induced null depends on inhibitor timing and whether the inhibitor persists long enough to change redistribution or release phases.
rows.localVsSystemic: This is currently a pharmacokinetic distribution question more than proven adipose-local CYP2D6 biology.
rows.boundary: Treat as an open tissue-local question, not as established CYP2D6 null phenotype.

rows.inheritedNullClaim: A systemic null model should ask whether CYP2D6 is locally expressed and whether local steroid, xenobiotic, or lipid-derived substrate handling is affected.
rows.inducedNullClaim: A medication-induced state should be modeled only if inhibitor distribution and local expression are plausible.
rows.localVsSystemic: Current v0 evidence is not enough to claim a specific reproductive or endocrine phenotype.
rows.boundary: Treat as an open tissue-local question, not as established CYP2D6 null phenotype.

rows.inheritedNullClaim: A true null may alter exposure to immune-active external substances or endogenous lipid mediators, but direct immune-cell CYP2D6 null biology is not established here.
rows.inducedNullClaim: Drug-induced CYP2D6 suppression can overlap with immune-active medications or inflammatory states, but mechanism needs source-specific review.
rows.localVsSystemic: This is a hypothesis layer for pathway load and exposure, not a clinical immune phenotype claim.
rows.boundary: Treat as an open tissue-local question, not as established CYP2D6 null phenotype.

rows.inheritedNullClaim: The systemic-null frame keeps these compartments open when expression or substrate evidence emerges.
rows.inducedNullClaim: Induced null status should not be projected into these tissues unless inhibitor distribution and local enzyme evidence support it.
rows.localVsSystemic: A whole-body genetic absence is conceptually different from a plasma drug interaction label, but each peripheral claim still needs evidence.
rows.boundary: Treat as an open tissue-local question, not as established CYP2D6 null phenotype.
